ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general snail food product class; no specific entry for Vietnamese bong snail with salted egg sauce. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers prepared snails broadly; no specific code for salted egg yolk sauce preparation. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of bong snail with salted egg sauce weighs approximately 300 g, representing a typical single-person serving as listed on food delivery platforms in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Snails, raw (FDC ID 175175); Egg yolk, salted (FDC ID 172185); Butter, without salt (FDC ID 173410); Milk, evaporated, canned, whole (FDC ID 171278)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for bong snail with salted egg sauce as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for raw snails (FDC ID 175175), salted egg yolk (FDC ID 172185), butter without salt (FDC ID 173410), and evaporated whole milk (FDC ID 171278), weighted by typical recipe proportions. Input per_100g values (provenance: grabfood, confidence 0.7) are consistent with this composite estimate and have been retained with cross-validation. Singapore HPB database consulted for salted egg yolk sauce dish context; FAO/INFOODS SEA table consulted for regional snail nutrient reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on mixed seafood dish with low-carbohydrate egg-based sauce
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bong snail with salted egg sauce. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the analogous category of mixed seafood stir-fry with egg-based sauce, referencing University of Sydney GI Database entries for low-carbohydrate seafood dishes and published GI values for egg-based sauces (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). GL per 300 g serving = (35 × 8.33 × 3.0) / 100 = 8.75. Classification is low-GI, consistent with protein-dominant, moderate-fat dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg/100g from salted duck egg yolks and sauce; low potassium and fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish as sodium reaches 400 mg/100g — equivalent to 1,200 mg per 300 g serving — representing 52–60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; request reduced salted egg yolks and avoid adding extra sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (8.33 g/100g) and low sugar (2.67 g/100g) offset by high cholesterol (150 mg/100g) and saturated fat (6.67 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish has a low estimated glycemic index (~35) and modest carbohydrate load, making it acceptable for blood glucose management in moderate portions; however, the high cholesterol (150 mg/100g) and saturated fat (6.67 g/100g) from salted egg yolks and butter elevate cardiovascular risk in type 2 diabetic patients and frequency of consumption should be limited.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 150 mg/100g, saturated fat 6.67 g/100g, trans fat 0.17 g/100g, and sodium 400 mg/100g from salted egg yolk and butter
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish sparingly: a single 300 g serving delivers approximately 450 mg cholesterol, 20 g saturated fat, and 1,200 mg sodium, all of which significantly exceed recommended daily limits and contribute to atherogenic risk; substituting butter with olive oil and reducing salted egg yolks would materially lower the cardiovascular burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.67 g/100g exceeds 5 g/100g RA threshold; butter excluded from MUFA/PUFA carve-out
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Saturated fat at 6.67 g/100g from butter and salted egg yolks exceeds the rheumatoid arthritis threshold of 5 g/100g and may upregulate TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines; patients with RA should substitute butter with extra-virgin olive oil and reduce the number of salted egg yolks used.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bong snail with salted egg sauce (Vietnamese: Ốc bông sốt trứng muối) is a Vietnamese-origin seafood dish popularised in Singapore's food delivery market. Bong snails — a variety of freshwater or brackish-water snail prized for their firm, slightly chewy texture — are stir-fried and coated in a rich, creamy salted egg yolk sauce made from salted duck egg yolks, butter, evaporated milk, curry leaves, chilli padi, and garlic. The sauce is intensely savoury, mildly spicy, and aromatic, with a golden-yellow colour characteristic of salted egg yolk preparations. The dish is served hot, typically as a shared plate or individual main course. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 217 kcal, 11.7 g protein, 15 g fat (6.7 g saturated), 8.3 g carbohydrate, and 400 mg sodium. The high sodium and saturated fat content from salted egg yolks and butter make this dish a high-concern food for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. The glycemic index is estimated at 35 (low), making it relatively safe for blood glucose management in moderate portions, though the cholesterol content (150 mg/100g) warrants caution for diabetic patients with cardiovascular comorbidities. The dish is gluten-free and reflects the salted egg yolk flavour trend prominent in Singaporean and Southeast Asian cuisine.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for bong snail with salted egg sauce as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for snails, salted egg yolk, butter, and evaporated milk, cross-validated against the input grabfood values (confidence 0.7). The GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese-Singaporean salted egg yolk snail dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB database, or FAO/INFOODS SEA table as a single composite entry as of 2026. Two duplicate portion entries (350 g and 300 g, both labelled '1 plate', both is_default=true) were present in the input; the 300 g value has been retained as the default serving size as it is the more conservative and commonly cited portion for this dish type on Singapore food delivery platforms. The dish is classified as gluten-free per input dietary_tags; however, cross-contamination risk exists in restaurant preparation environments. Halal status is unconfirmed as butter and evaporated milk sourcing is unknown. Chinese local names (simplified: 螺肉咸蛋酱; traditional: 螺肉鹹蛋醬) are descriptive transliterations reflecting the dish components (snail meat, salted egg sauce) as no standardised Chinese name exists for this specific Vietnamese-Singaporean preparation. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names are functional descriptive translations added to satisfy G6 multilingual requirements for this Singapore-served dish.
